title: Goat kid: the first days
subtitle: What to do in the first 3 days after a kid is born.
excerpt: The first three days set the trajectory for a kid's health. Colostrum in the first hours, a dry warm environment, and a bond with the doe are the three things that matter most. Getting them right is straightforward; missing any one of them creates problems that are hard to recover.
sourceNotes: British Goat Society, kid rearing guidance. NADIS, goat kid care factsheet. SAC Consulting, goat health notes.

glossaryTerms.definition: Removal of the horn bud in young kids, usually within the first week of life, to prevent horn growth. Requires a vet in the UK;it is a painful procedure and is not legal without anaesthesia. Not the same as dehorning (removal of established horns in adult animals), which is a more invasive procedure.

content.text: Goat kids are born with minimal fat reserves and lose heat rapidly in cold or wet conditions. The sequence in the first hour matters: clear the airway if needed, dry and warm the kid, treat the navel, ensure colostrum intake. All four steps should happen within the first 30 to 60 minutes.

If the doe is attentive, let her lick the kid dry; this stimulates breathing, dries the coat, and starts the bonding process. If the doe is not attending to the kid, rub it firmly with a clean towel, paying particular attention to the chest and face. Check the nostrils are clear. A vigorous rub will also stimulate the kid to lift its head and call, which triggers the doe's bonding response.

Spray the navel cord stump with iodine spray within the first 15 to 20 minutes of birth. This is as important in kids as in lambs; navel ill (infection ascending the cord to the joints and liver) is common and serious. Repeat the application once more at the next check.

Kids need colostrum within the first two to four hours of life. Check the doe's teats are clear; first-time does sometimes have a wax plug in the teat canal. If the kid is not suckling by two hours, strip a small amount of colostrum from the doe and feed it by bottle or stomach tube. Kids that miss colostrum in the first six hours are significantly more susceptible to infection throughout their lives.

A kid born in cold conditions should be housed in a deep-bedded pen with a heat lamp overhead. A shivering kid that is not suckling needs warming before anything else; the gut does not absorb nutrients well when the body temperature is low. Dry, warm, then feed.

Check the kid is passing meconium (dark, sticky first droppings) within 12 hours of birth. Failure to pass meconium within 24 hours indicates a blockage and needs a vet. Monitor urination; a kid straining to urinate or showing swelling around the navel needs immediate veterinary attention. By day two a well-fed kid will be running, jumping, and investigating its surroundings.
If you want to disbud kids, arrange a vet visit in the first week.
is only legal in the UK with veterinary involvement and anaesthesia. Contact your vet before kidding to arrange timing.
